title: PQC for the Curious — Visual Tour
whatToExpect: Plain-language tour of post-quantum cryptography — no prior background needed
See one quantum threat, one milestone, three algorithm names, one in-browser demo
PQC 101 module shown via the Visual tab only — pictures over equations

steps.title: A 26-minute visual tour
steps.whyItMatters: Get the big picture without needing a technical background.
tasks: Watch the workshop play through
Pause whenever you want to read more
steps.expectedOutput: A clear mental picture of why PQC matters and what is being built.
steps.narration: This is the curious-explorer tour. We will visit a few pages, see one threat, one milestone, three algorithm names, and run one in-browser demo. No code, no equations — pictures and plain language.

cues.text: A 26-minute visual tour — pictures over equations, plain language throughout.

steps.title: Confirm Curious persona
steps.whyItMatters: Curious mode shows visual content and hides technical-only modules.
tasks: Confirm role = Curious
Confirm proficiency = Curious
steps.narration: Curious mode reshapes the platform — visual diagrams replace tables, advanced modules stay hidden.

cues.text: Persona definition — one pick is enough for the curious tour.
Opening the persona wizard.
Curious track — visual content, plain language, no further setup needed.
That's it — Curious skips region and industry. The platform shows visual content tuned for newcomers.

steps.title: Orient on the landing page
steps.whyItMatters: A 30-second orient — what this platform is and what each section does.
tasks: Open the page
Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with Orient on the landing page
steps.narration: The landing page is the home base. It tracks your journey, surfaces upcoming milestones, and links to every section. Take a moment to orient.

cues.text: PQC Today — your guide to post-quantum cryptography. Built for every level of expertise.
After your persona, three CTAs appear: Video Tour, Browse Workshops, and Start Your Journey. Pick the one that fits.

steps.title: One quantum threat — what it does
steps.whyItMatters: See a single concrete threat — what it does and who it affects.
tasks: Open the page
Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with One quantum threat — what it does
steps.narration: The /threats page lists every quantum-related threat we track. We will look at just one to get the idea — what it does, who it affects, when.

cues.text: Quantum threats — risks the cryptography we use today might face soon.
Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later: an attacker saves encrypted traffic today, decrypts it years from now once they own a quantum computer.
This is why long-lived secrets need PQC protection today, not when CRQC arrives.

steps.title: One major milestone on the timeline
steps.whyItMatters: See one global milestone to get a sense of the pace of change.
tasks: Open the page
Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with One major milestone on the timeline
steps.narration: The /timeline page lists every regulatory and industry milestone. We will spot just one to get a sense of how fast the industry is moving.

cues.text: The migration timeline — milestones from governments, standards bodies, and industry leaders.
NIST published its first three PQC standards in 2024. Now organisations have until 2030–2035 to adopt.
Hover any event for source links — every milestone is backed by a primary document.

steps.title: Three NIST PQC algorithm names
steps.whyItMatters: Meet the three algorithms NIST has standardized. You only need to remember the names.
tasks: Open the page
Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with Three NIST PQC algorithm names
steps.narration: Three algorithm names you'll keep hearing — ML-KEM (key exchange), ML-DSA (signing), SLH-DSA (signing with extra-conservative security). That's the core PQC vocabulary.

cues.text: NIST PQC algorithms — three names to remember.
M L K E M, the key exchange algorithm. Standardised as FIPS 203. It replaces RSA and Diffie-Hellman.
M L D S A, standardised as FIPS 204. And S L H D S A, standardised as FIPS 205. Both handle digital signatures, replacing RSA and ECDSA.

steps.title: Make a key — interactive ML-KEM demo
steps.whyItMatters: Click a button, watch a quantum-safe key get generated in your browser.
tasks: Open the page
Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with Make a key — interactive ML-KEM demo
steps.narration: The /playground lets you try the new algorithms without writing code. We'll just generate one ML-KEM key — see what the public key looks like.

cues.text: The playground — try every PQC algorithm in your browser. No installation, no code.
Find the ML-KEM section. The "Generate Keys" button creates a quantum-safe keypair.
The public key shown is what your browser would send to a server in a quantum-safe TLS handshake.

steps.title: PQC 101 — Visual tab only
steps.whyItMatters: A picture-first walk through the basics. Visual tab keeps it intuitive.
tasks: Open the page
Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with PQC 101 — Visual tab only
steps.narration: We'll open the PQC 101 module on its Visual tab — diagrams and animations only, no equations or technical text.

cues.text: PQC 101 — the visual primer. Diagrams and animations explain the basics.
See how a quantum computer breaks RSA — and how lattice-based cryptography resists it.
Visual is the easiest tab for newcomers. Other tabs go deeper if you're curious.

steps.title: Trust and transparency
steps.whyItMatters: Who built this, where the data comes from, why you can trust it.
tasks: Open the page
Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.narration: The /about page explains who built this platform, where the data comes from, and why every claim is sourced.

cues.text: About PQC Today — open source, transparent sources, no vendor pitch.
Every claim links back to a primary source — RFCs, NIST docs, vendor whitepapers.

steps.title: Quiz — test what you learned
steps.whyItMatters: A 5-to-45-minute quiz with persona-tuned topics gives you a quick measure of what stuck.
tasks: Note the duration slider and difficulty filter
See how persona-tuned topics are pre-selected
Run the quiz yourself after the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: A working knowledge of the quiz interface; you'll come back to actually take it.
steps.narration: The quiz is the quickest way to check what you've absorbed. Pick how much time you have, pick the topics that matter, and get a scored breakdown by category and difficulty.

cues.text: Test what you learned. Pick a duration, pick topics, go.
Duration slider. Five to forty-five minutes. Quiz scales the question count to match.
Difficulty filter. Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced. Multi-select.
Topic grid. Categories pre-filtered by your persona context.
Selected topics show a checkmark. The Start button activates once you pick at least one.
Start the quiz when you're ready. We'll skip running it inside the workshop.
Results show your score, category breakdown, difficulty breakdown, and let you retake or change topics.

steps.title: Three things to remember
steps.whyItMatters: Three takeaways you can drop into a coffee chat this week.
tasks: Remember: HNDL, NIST 2024, ML-KEM/ML-DSA
steps.expectedOutput: Three sentences you can repeat to a colleague.
steps.narration: Three things to remember — the threat (HNDL), the timeline (NIST standards published 2024), and the names (ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA). That is the curious-explorer takeaway.

cues.text: Three things to remember. The threat is called Harvest Now Decrypt Later. NIST published the new standards in 2024. The two names you will hear are M L K E M and M L D S A.
